pages_preview.text_preview: Metaketa III: Natural Resource Governance Pre-meta-analysis Plan Version registered at EGAP. ∗ Miriam Golden#7 Daniel L. Nielson#7 Daniel Rubenson#7 Tara Slough#4,7 Donald Green#7 Francisco Alpizar#6 Maria Bernedo#6 Zhang Bing#5 Mark Buntaine#5 Darin Christensen#3 Alicia Cooperman#1 Sabrina Eisenbarth#2 Paul Ferraro#6 Louis Graham#2 Alexandra Hartman#3 Chaning Jang#2 Ariana Keyman#2 Jacob Kopas#4 Alexandra Richey#1 Anouk Rigterink#2 Cyrus Samii#3 Brigitte Seim#1 Johannes Urpelainen#4 Susan Hyde#7 November 6, 2017 Abstract We describe our plan for a meta-analysis of results of six studies on the impact of commu- nity monitoring on the governance of renewable natural resources. The studies were designed to operate as much as possible according to common protocols and to generate comparable outcomes. ∗Authorannotationsare: #1Brazilstudy, #2Ugandastudy, #3Liberiastudy, #4Perustudy, #5Chinastudy, #6 Costa Rica/Nicaragua study, #7 Metaketa steering committee

In this document we describe the research and analysis strategy for a Metaketa sponsored by Evidence in Governance and Politics (EGAP) on community monitoring of governance of forests and water. First we outline the motivation for a Metaketa on this topic and describe the Metaketa approachtolearning. Wethenprovidetheoreticalbackgroundanddescribethecommontreatment. Next we enumerate our hypotheses and provide details on operationalization and measurement. A sectionoutlinesthemeta-analysis, includingourplanstocollectandincorporatepriorbeliefsabout treatment effects. We conclude by discussing our approach to ethics and describing the timing of the research. 1 Motivation Human communities have been confronting and resolving collective natural resource problems for centuries using a variety of governance mechanisms (Ostrom, 1990). However, modern technolo- gies, booming populations, growing international economic transactions, and burgeoning resource demands have created new challenges for natural resource management. These transformations, in addition to climate change, push natural resource issues to the forefront of the scientific and social scientific research agenda. We ask if natural resource governance improves when communities monitor resource use. Given that natural resources are usually subject to government regulations that affect their use, community-basedmonitoringrepresentsonewaytocollectinformationaboutwhethertheseregula- tions are being observed and more generally whether resource use is appropriate. Using monitoring techniques that have already been developed and implemented elsewhere in each country of study, the interventions in this Metaketa all establish new community-based monitoring activities in lo- cales where community monitoring of the specific natural resource did not previously occur on a regular basis. This allows study of whether site-appropriate monitoring techniques cause positive changes in natural resource governance outcomes. Community monitoring has long been suggested as a key method of governing the commons (Ostrom, 1990). In some realms, such as health care, evidence from a prominent randomized trial suggests that community monitoring has large effects (Bj¨orkman and Svensson, 2009), although whether these results replicate is still unknown (Posner, Parkerson and Raffler, 2016). In other contexts, community monitoring appears to have modest results (Lassibille et al., 2010) or even none at all (Olken, 2007). Whether citizen monitoring in the area of natural resources affects resource outcomes remains an open question (Conrad and Hilchey, 2011). Assembling evidence to evaluate this systematically is the purpose of this Metaketa. 2 The Metaketa Approach Metaketas areintegratedresearchprogramsinwhichmultipleteamsofresearchersworkindifferent sites on projects in parallel to generate generalizable answers to major questions of scholarly and policy importance. The core pillars of the Metaketa approach are: 1. Major themes: Metaketas tackle major questions of scholarly and policy relevance, with a focus on consolidation of knowledge rather than theoretical innovation. 2. Strong designs: all studies employ randomized interventions to identify causal effects. 1

3. Collaboration and autonomy: teams work on parallel projects and collaborate on core features of research design, although projects may produce different results. 4. Comparable interventions: because of the collaborative design, differences in results should be attributable to factors other than the intervention. 5. Comparable measures: implementation of each study naturally adjusts to the context, meaning that any similar results do not depend on implementation details. 6. Analytic transparency: research teams share a common commitment to analytic trans- parency, including design registration and pre-analysis, open data and materials, and third- party analysis prior to publication. 7. Formal synthesis: the meta-analysis of the studies is based on ex-ante planning and inte- grated publication to avoid file-drawer bias. The Natural Resource Governance (NRG) Metaketa was launched in Spring 2016 and will run until Fall 2019, including analysis and write-up. This Metaketa is administered by the Evidence in Governance and Politics (EGAP) network, housed at the University of California, Berkeley. MetaketaadministrationisconductedbyaSteeringCommitteeappointedbyEGAP.Theobjectives are to implement a series of closely-coordinated experimental projects that assesses the role of communitymonitoringonnaturalresourceuse, onusersatisfactionwithuse, andontheknowledge andresourcestewardshipoftheaffectedcommunities. Theprojectsareconductedbylocally-expert teams of academic researchers. In this document, we interchangeably refer to these as “research teams” or “Principal Investigators” (PIs). PIs work in their countries with local organizations, typically non-governmental organizations, which we call “local partners.” 3 The Policy Problem In its documents outlining the Reduced Emissions from Deforestation and Degradation (REDD+), theUnitedNationsFrameworkConventiononClimateChange(UNFCCC)callsfortheinvolvement of local users in assessments of resource threats to forests (discussed in Danielson et al. (2013)). Globally, community monitoring is the standard mechanism that is used to involve local stakehold- ers in resource threat assessments for forests as well as for other replenishable natural resources, such as water. The six projects in this Metaketa operate in communities that currently experience substantial threats to a specific renewable resource (either water or forests). Threats come from multiple sources, including changing climatic conditions. We focus specifically on human governance of these resources, meaning human activities that affect the quality or quantity of the resource in question and coordinated, explicitly-delineated human solutions to these problems. The national and threat contexts of our studies are purposefully diverse, but the nature of the governance threats to communal natural resources follows two possible logics. First, “internal threats” to resources emerge from users within the local community and typically take the form of collective action problems. Second, “external threats” to a community’s natural resource come from non- community members who predate upon the community’s resources. The projects in our study operate in communities that face internal, external, or both types of threats. 2

3.1 Internal (Within Community) Threats Internalthreatstoacommunity’snaturalresourcesderivefromcollectiveactionproblems, meaning a local failure to coordinate behavior around appropriate levels or types of resource use. The problem of collective action is well known and has been explained and formalized in classic works on threats to common pool resources (CPR) (Hardin, 1968; Olson, 1971; Ostrom, 1990). Common pool resources are natural or man-made resources whose characteristics make it impossible or very difficult to exclude specific users, including those who do not contribute to upkeep or who overdraw the resource. In the context of a common pool resource — in our studies, forests or water — individual users maximize personal consumption/misuse the resource, to the detriment of the overall resource supply or quality, and these individuals cannot reasonably be prevented from resource access and use. Existingliteratureprovidesampleevidenceofthepresenceofcollectiveactionproblems(internal threats) with respect to the management of CPR alongside a variety of prescriptions for how to mitigate these problems. Three broad classes of solutions dominate the literature: (i) privatization; (ii) external regulation of resource use and (local or external) enforcement of those regulations and (iii) community-based resource governance in the form of well-observed locally-coordinated usage prescriptions(Ostrom,1990). Inoursettings, privatizationofthecommonresourceisexcludedand our interventions are not aimed at encouraging or establishing privatization. Persistent internal threats to the resource suggest some failure in governance of either or both of the latter two types: self-regulation and/or enforcement and sanctioning. We focus attention on the latter two. 3.2 External (Extra-Community) Threats Naturalresourcesoftenrepresentconsiderablefinancialvalueonnationalandinternationalmarkets, making theft or other forms of illegal use an attractive option. Precisely because of the financial incentivesinvolved,agentsexternaltotheusercommunitiesmayillegallyuseorremovetheresource in question. External actors may operate independently of local actors. However, there may also be collusion with members of the local community, who are susceptible to payment schemes that embroil them in illegal activities. Members of the local community are useful to external predators because of the former’s local knowledge of the resource and of the setting, knowledge that external actors need but do not have. Theliteraturetendstofocusonbetterauthoritativeidentification, regulation, enforcement, and sanctioning to reduce the threat represented by external resource predators. In addition, to the extent that local users collude with external actors, community coordination and social sanctioning may also be relevant in reducing such collusion. Whether the threat is internal or external, the two conduits that we identify for possible miti- gation are thus community coordination and self-regulation or authoritative enforcement via sanc- tions. As we describe below, the six interventions that operate under the auspices of the NRG Metaketa are all designed to target one or both of these aspects of governance. 4 Theoretical Background and Literature Community monitoring involves recruiting resource users from the affected local community and deploying them on a regular basis to observe, record and disseminate information about patterns 3
